summary: Canary p95 latency spiked to 2.1s during first 60s after rollout, stable pods unaffected.
root_cause: JVM/interpreter cold start, no code defect.
resolution: Promoted after latency normalized within 2 minutes.

summary: Canary error rate hit 27% on POST /orders, latency normal, stable pods showed zero errors.
root_cause: Genuine regression introduced in new code path.
resolution: Rolled back immediately, error traced to a null check removed in the diff.

summary: Canary latency elevated 15% above stable, error rate unchanged, gradually returned to baseline over 5 minutes.
root_cause: Downstream Redis connection pool warm-up, expected behavior.
resolution: Held for 5 minutes, then promoted once latency matched stable.

summary: Canary latency roughly 100x stable (2s vs 0.02s), consistent across every request, no recovery over 10 minutes.
root_cause: Blocking sleep/wait accidentally left in a code path, or a synchronous call to a dependency that should have been async.
resolution: Rolled back immediately; sustained order-of-magnitude latency gap with no recovery trend is never a warm-up artifact.

summary: Canary error rate 2%, stable error rate 1.8%, both within normal background noise for this endpoint.
root_cause: No regression; both tracks show the same baseline error rate from unrelated client-side retry behavior.
resolution: Promoted; error rate difference was not statistically meaningful.

summary: Canary error rate spiked to 40% for exactly 90 seconds then dropped to 0%, latency unaffected throughout.
root_cause: Transient downstream dependency (Redis) restart during a rolling update, unrelated to the canary code itself.
resolution: Held during the spike, promoted once error rate stayed at 0% for 5 consecutive minutes.

summary: Canary latency 3x stable and error rate 15%, both elevated simultaneously and trending worse over time.
root_cause: Memory leak in new code causing garbage collection pressure, degrading both latency and reliability together.
resolution: Rolled back; combined and worsening latency + error signals are a strong regression indicator.

summary: Canary latency identical to stable, zero errors, all metrics nominal after 10 minutes of traffic.
resolution: Promoted immediately; clean signals across the board.

summary: Canary error rate 8%, all failures traced to a single downstream third-party API returning 503s, stable pods calling the same API show identical 8% failure rate.
root_cause: Upstream third-party outage, not a canary defect; both tracks equally affected.
resolution: Promoted; error source was external and symmetric across stable and canary.

summary: Canary latency 12% higher than stable during a traffic spike, returned to parity once traffic normalized.
root_cause: Canary running fewer replicas than stable, so it saturated sooner under the same load per-pod; not a code defect.
resolution: Held through the traffic spike, promoted once latency matched stable at normal load.

summary: Canary error rate 35%, all failures are 500s with the same stack trace pointing at a null pointer in newly added validation logic, stable shows 0% errors.
root_cause: Genuine code regression: missing null check in new validation path.
resolution: Rolled back; identical, reproducible errors isolated to the new code.

summary: Canary and stable both show a brief latency bump to 2x baseline for about 30 seconds, correlated with a cluster-wide node autoscaling event.
root_cause: Infrastructure-level scheduling disruption affecting all pods equally, unrelated to the canary release.
resolution: Held briefly, promoted once both tracks returned to baseline together.

summary: Canary latency steadily increasing over 15 minutes, now at 4x stable and still climbing, no plateau.
root_cause: Unbounded queue or connection buildup in new code, a slow-building resource exhaustion bug.
resolution: Rolled back before full exhaustion; a steadily worsening trend with no plateau is a strong rollback signal even before it becomes catastrophic.

summary: Canary shows a single isolated error out of 500 requests, latency nominal, no repeat occurrences in the following 10 minutes.
root_cause: One-off transient blip, likely a single dropped connection; not reproducible.
resolution: Promoted; an isolated singleton error with no recurrence is noise, not a regression.

summary: Canary error rate 22% concentrated entirely on POST requests, GET requests show 0% errors on canary, stable shows 0% on both.
root_cause: Regression specific to the write path introduced in the new release, read path unaffected.
resolution: Rolled back; a large, endpoint-specific error rate isolated to canary is a clear regression even when it doesn't affect every endpoint.

summary: Canary latency and error rate both nominal for the first 3 minutes, then errors jump to 18% right as a scheduled batch job begins running on the same node.
root_cause: Resource contention with an unrelated batch job sharing the node, not a canary code issue.
resolution: Held until the batch job completed, promoted once errors returned to 0% afterward.

summary: Canary latency 1.5x stable, error rate 0%, signal has been stable (neither improving nor worsening) for 8 minutes.
root_cause: Unclear; a mild, persistent latency regression without errors, not yet explained by warm-up or infra causes.
resolution: Held for further observation rather than promoting or rolling back on ambiguous data; escalated for manual review after 15 minutes if unresolved.
